in the LMIHIP, there is no "legally enforceable contract or agreement" for the

expenditure of Housing Funds for any legitimate housing purpose. A contract for the

construction of the overpasses does not constitute such a "legally enforceable

contract" since it is not a contract for a legitimate housing purpose.

The LMIHIP effectively removes $24 million of Housing Funds from the

Agency's available resources, yet does not expend those monies for housing

purposes. See Sections IV.A., B. and C., supra. These "hidden" monies should be

considered in the determination of the Agency's "excess surplus." Yet is it clear from

the LMIHIP that the Agency does not intend to account for the $24 million in that

manner.

E. STRADINGS MULTIPLE REPRESENTATION AS THE CITY

ATTORNEY, AGENCY SPECIAL COUNSEL AND AS BOND COUNSEL

FOR THE TAX ALLOCATION NOTES CONSTITUTES A CONFLICT OF

INTEREST RENDERING THE TAX ALLOCATION NOTES VOID

The multiple representation of the Agency by Strading, Yocca, Carlson &

Rauth ("Strading") as City Attorney, Agency general counsel and bond counsel in

connection with the issuance of the Tax Allocation Notes contravenes the conflict of

interest provisions of Government Code § 1090 ("§ 1090"), which prohibit officers and

employees of a district from being financially interested in any contract made by them

in their official capacity. The purpose of the statute is to eliminate relationships

fraught with temptation, avoid the appearance of impropriety and assure the

government entity of the officer's undivided loyalty and uncompromised allegiance.

Thomson v. Call, 38 Cal.3d 633, 647-648 (1985), cert. denied, 474 U.S. 1957 (1986).

For each of the foregoing reasons, Dibley respectfully requests that this Court

enter Summary Judgment in favor of Interested Person Dolores Dibley, invalidate the

V.

STRADLING'S FINANCIAL INTEREST IN THE TAX ALLOCATION NOTES

VOIDS THOSE CONTRACTS AND RELATED DOCUMENTS

The Agency's Opposition papers do nothing to detract from the clear

decisional authority demonstrating that Stradling (1) is a "public official"; (2) has a

financial interest in the success or failure of the'Tax Allocation Notes; and

(3) participated in the "making" of those notes and related documents. As such,

pursuant to Government Code § 1090, the transaction is void.

As was reasoned in [illegible]

Many district attorneys and city attorneys are permitted to have a

private practice. However, where his advice is properly sought by the

governing body, the district attorney or city attorney may not acquire a direct

financial interest in a contract which is made by his employing governing body.

in "making" the Tax Allocation Notes [and related documents, including the LMIHIP]

FPPC Regulations], not the payment for legal services.

"Rodriguez Letter"] at 1. Moreover, despite the circumstances under which the

Agency claims the more recent Rodriguez Letter was requested, the substance of the

Rodriguez Letter makes clear that a private firm who acts as both Agency counsel

and bond counsel has a prohibited financial interest in the bonds or notes to be

issued. The Rodriguez Letter found, for example, that special counsel who is also

On behalf of our client, the Adelanto Improvement Agency, the
redevelopment agency for the City of Adelanto, we are seeking an
opinion from the Fair Political Practices Commission ("FPPC") as to
the following issues:

1. Is it a conflict of interest for a law firm which serves
as a special counsel to a redevelopment agency to also
serve as bond counsel to that agency where the amount of
its compensation as bond counsel is based in part on the
size or success of the bond issue?

2. Is it a conflict of interest for a law firm which serves
as a contract-City Attorney to also serve as bond counsel
to that city's redevelopment agency, where the amount of
its compensation as bond counsel is based in part on the
size or success of the bond issue?

3. Does a contingent fee arrangement for bond counsel
[whether or not bond counsel also represents the
redevelopment agency or city] constitute a forbidden
material financial interest?

Deanne Stone
November 6, 1992
Page 2


We understand that the City of Lancaster ("Lancaster") and the
California Association of Bond Lawyers" ("CABL") are asking for
a formal opinion from this body concerning certain conflict of
interest questions that arise out of multiple simultaneous
representation of public bodies, especially in the area of bond
counsel. We appreciate this opportunity to identify and discuss
certain key issues on this same topic.

We have advised our client that there is an inherent conflict
in having a law firm which provides general legal advice to a
development agency [or city] also simultaneously to provide bond
counsel services, especially where compensation for bond counsel
services are [in part] dependent upon the size or success of the
bond issuance. The basis for our rationale, in addition to the
specific statutory provisions discussed in greater detail below,
stem in part from notions of common-sense. There are certain
issues which might be answered one way by counsel on behalf of the
agency and another if by independent bond counsel. One example,
discussed below, occurred in Lancaster: the validation action
tangentially referred to in Lancaster's letter is based upon a
challenge to the propriety of using funds restricted for Low- and
Moderate-Income Housing to secure $30 million in bonds issued to
construct general public improvements; i.e. railway overpasses. In
Lancaster, the law firm [which acted as City Attorney and Agency
General Counsel as well as bond counsel] actively participated in
drafting the program to use those restricted funds in this way. We
think that an independent bond counsel might have asked serious
questions about the propriety of this use, perhaps forestalling the
need for the validation action.

We also think that there is a question as to the legitimacy of
"contingent fee" arrangements for bond counsel where bond counsel
participates in the determination of the size of the bond issue.

during the pendency of the validation action mentioned above, the

other revenues to proceed with one of two overpass deposits the
litigation; it further indicated in court papers that it may use
these same bond proceeds to undertake other, unspecified
improvements rather than construct the overpasses. This poses a

The dual representation ot the Agency by Stradling, Yocca, Carlson & Rauth
('SYC&R) as Agency general counsel and bond counsel in connection with the

temptation, avoid the appearance of impropriety and assure the government entity of
the officer's undivided loyalty and uncompromised allegiance. Thomson v. Call, 38
Cal.3d 633, 647-648 (1985), cert. denied,474 U.S. 1957 (1986). ln furtherance of

A contract for the construction of the overpasses does not constitute such a "legally enforceable contract" since it is not a contract for a legitimate housing purpose. The LMIHIP effectively removes $24 million of Housing Funds from the Agency's available resources, yet does not expend those monies for housing purposes. See Sections IV.A., B. and C., supra. These "hidden" monies should be considered in the determination of the Agency's "excess surplus." Yet is it clear from the LMIHIP that the Agency does not intend to account for the $24 million in that manner. E.
